Light, slightly suspenseful instrumental music begins as a professional female narrator announces Firefly's plan for its next flight in early 2022. Tom Markusic, Founder and CEO of Firefly Aerospace, is then seen in a workshop with large rocket components, speaking directly to the camera about their goal to have the vehicle ready by year-end for an early new year launch. The camera pans across the workshop, revealing a long black rocket body and a large circular section, before focusing on a woman with curly hair who, speaking in an energetic, clear female voice, emphasizes that "now it's about refinement," making each flight progressively easier, with plans already extending to flights six and seven. An animated graphic of Earth with orbiting satellites appears, displaying Firefly Aerospace's logo and cost details, as the female narrator explains that Firefly's Alpha rocket will compete in the small to medium launch market, taking payloads like small satellites to low Earth orbit for $15 million. The graphic then expands to compare Firefly with competitors like Rocket Lab, Astra, and ABL, detailing their respective costs and payload capacities, while the narrator lists Rocket Lab's Electron at $7 million, Astra's at $2.5 million, and ABL's RS1 at $12 million. segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a detailed infographic comparing space companies' costs and payload capacities for low-Earth orbit missions, highlighting Firefly Aerospace's $15M per launch for up to 1,000 kg, while a female voice provides context, noting SpaceX's Falcon 9 costs about $62M per launch. The scene transitions to a graphic showcasing Firefly’s rocket lineup, including the Firefly Alpha and the larger Firefly Beta, depicted as sleek blue rockets, with the female voice explaining the Beta's expected capacity of up to 8,000 kg. A technical diagram of the Firefly Beta then appears, displaying its specifications, followed by a conceptual rendering of the futuristic, reusable Firefly Gamma rocket plane. Michael Sheetz, a man in a dark suit, then speaks directly to the camera in a well-lit living room, stating that strategic partnerships will enable them to start flying hardware in as little as a couple of years. The video cuts to an engineer working on a 3D model of a spacecraft on dual monitors, as the female voice mentions Firefly's additional plans for a winged reusable rocket. Tom Markusic, Founder and CEO of Firefly Aerospace, is then seen seated in a workshop surrounded by large rocket components, explaining that these long-term development projects won't progress until their core essential businesses are established. While Markusic remains on screen, the female voice adds that Firefly is positioning itself as an "end-to-end space transportation company," a point Markusic further clarifies, stating they are not just a rocket company, but that rockets are an important part, representing one-third of their entire space transportation architecture. The scene shifts to a model of the Blue Ghost lunar lander in a cleanroom, complete with solar panels and landing legs, as eerie, atmospheric background music begins to play. The segment concludes with a graphic of the Blue Ghost lander on the Moon, detailing its commercially available resources for a 2023 mission, while Tom Markusic's voice returns, describing Blue Ghost as their lunar lander solution, before the audio cuts off. The CNBC logo remains visible in the top right corner throughout.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a wide shot of the 'Blue Ghost' lunar lander prototype, a black and gold structure with extended solar panels and landing legs, displayed in a clean, well-lit industrial workshop. A male voice, identified as Tom Markusic, Founder and CEO of Firefly Aerospace, explains that the lander fulfills the NASA commercial lunar payload services mission, having won their first proposal at $93 million to carry 10 payloads, just under 100 kilograms worth of NASA experiments. The scene transitions to Tom Markusic, wearing a black 'Blue Ghost' t-shirt, seated in the workshop and speaking directly to the camera. A female narrator then states that NASA's Artemis program aims to return humans to the Moon, and projects like Blue Ghost will deliver scientific research to the surface. This is followed by a NASA animation showing a large rocket being transported on a launch pad, then a detailed animation of astronauts on the lunar surface near a lander and rover with an American flag planted. A different male voice, identified as Michael Sheetz, discusses how Blue Origin, SpaceX, and others are working on the human lander solution, and these precursor missions are smaller science missions to understand that environment better. The video cuts to a close-up of a lander descending to the Moon with thrusters firing, then to a large spacecraft being assembled inside a NASA hangar, as Michael Sheetz continues, noting that this program represents NASA's push to becoming more of a customer rather than a manufacturer itself, and Firefly isn't the only one to win a contract, with awards steadily in the $70 to $100 million range. The scene shifts to Michael Sheetz, in a dark suit, speaking from a well-lit living room with large windows, followed by a shot of engineers working on a spacecraft in a workshop. The video returns to Michael Sheetz in the living room, then shows close-ups of the lander's solar panels and top-mounted sensors. The final scene features Bethany Baldwin-Pulcini, Systems Engineer at Firefly Aerospace, seated in the workshop with the lander in the background, speaking to the camera, describing payloads that are studying the lunar regolith and soil, performing science experiments on the way to the Moon and once on the lunar surface, and some that are studying the magnetic field. The CNBC logo is consistently visible in the top right corner throughout the segment.

audio_updated_entities.Bethany Baldwin-Pulcini: Bethany Baldwin-Pulcini, Systems Engineer at Firefly Aerospace, a woman with long dark hair and glasses, wearing a gray cardigan over a black top, seated in a workshop with the Blue Ghost lander in the background, speaking to the camera. She details payloads designed to study lunar regolith and soil, perform science experiments en route to and on the lunar surface, and investigate magnetic fields.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens in a well-lit workshop, where Bethany Baldwin-Pulcini, a Systems Engineer at Firefly Aerospace, wearing a gray cardigan and glasses, actively points to and explains various components of the Blue Ghost lunar lander prototype. She details gold-colored 3D-printed brackets, a solar panel array, and a spherical silver retroreflector, noting how it helps get "accurate distances to the lunar surface." She highlights a "coolest" payload called LMS, describing its probes that "shoot out and land on the lunar surface" to measure its magnetic field. The camera zooms in on intricate details like a gold lattice bracket and a circular mirror-like sensor. A male voice then emphasizes the importance of "weight savings" in design, stating "every kilogram is worth about a million dollars," and explains using "generative design" with computer algorithms to optimize the structure for survival. The scene dramatically transitions to a rocket launch, showing a tall black and white rocket with the 'FIREFLY' logo igniting and ascending into a clear blue sky, followed by a nighttime shot silhouetted against a bright flame trail. A female narrator states the company plans to launch in 2023 using a "SpaceX Falcon 9 rocket." The male voice returns, discussing the timeline for "building the flight hardware" and a "good six months to test it" before flight. The video cuts back to the workshop, where the gold thermal-insulated Blue Ghost lander, with extended landing legs, is displayed on a wheeled platform. A close-up of a textured, cratered surface resembling the Moon’s surface reinforces the lander’s destination, all while the CNBC logo remains visible in the top right corner.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a textured, cratered surface resembling the Moon, transitioning to an expansive outdoor shot of a gravel-covered field with industrial buildings, as Ray Allensworth, Deputy Program Manager at Firefly Aerospace, explains that they've "recreated the lunar surface for simulations." The scene shifts to an indoor workshop where Allensworth, seated with a golden spacecraft and lander components behind her, continues to speak about testing like they fly, using the "lunar lander sim" for their vision navigation system to "eliminate as many unknowns as possible." A dramatic shift then shows a SpaceX Falcon 9 rocket on a launch pad, igniting with smoke and fire, followed by the Dragon capsule in orbit, while a narrator explains that Tom Markusic, while at SpaceX, witnessed the development of the Dragon capsule program for high-revenue resupply missions. The narrative returns to the Firefly Aerospace workshop, where Tom Markusic, Founder and CEO, seated on a stool with large rocket sections in the background, speaks about how "the rocket gives you the keys to space" and that "Blue Ghost fully loaded with payload can generate about $150 million of revenue." He further details that "Alpha," the launcher, is about "$15 million retail per flight." A CNBC logo briefly appears. The video then showcases the black and gold Blue Ghost lunar lander prototype with extended solar panels and landing legs, followed by the Firefly rocket on a launch pad under a clear blue sky. The narrator adds that Firefly is also competing for rover projects and developing a "space utility vehicle." This is immediately followed by a screen displaying Firefly’s Space Utility Vehicle (SUV) with technical details, as Tom Markusic describes it as the "do everything utility vehicle, the Swiss Army knife of spacecraft." The segment concludes with interviews in the workshop, featuring Tom Markusic and Lauren Lyons, who is briefly heard mentioning "having the spacecraft," with rocket components visible behind them, all set to a consistent background music track.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a woman in a dark zip-up top speaking animatedly in a workshop, a large rocket section visible behind her, as she discusses how objects that 'can just hang out in orbit and transport things, that can service things, that can relay things' will be 'really big for us'. Upbeat background music swells as the scene transitions to dramatic nighttime and daytime rocket launches, featuring intense flames and smoke. A narrator states that 'launches are picking up', with 114 orbital launches in 2020, 41 of which were commercial. Next, Chad Anderson, a man in a white shirt, appears in a modern office with a space helmet on a stand behind him, explaining that we are in an 'entrepreneurial space age' moving into a 'SpaceX age' when Starship comes online, dramatically reducing costs from '$100,000 per kilogram' in the shuttle era to '$1,000-ish dollars a kilogram' with the Falcon Heavy, and ultimately to '$10 a kilogram' with Starship. The visuals then show a group observing a SpaceX rocket on a launch pad, a close-up of a rocket under the sun, and a fiery space shuttle launch. Engineers in blue suits are seen working on a satellite in a cleanroom with Korean text. A CNBC article headline appears, stating 'Bank of America expects the space industry to triple to a $1.4 trillion market within a decade', with the narrator confirming the global space economy is already worth over $423 billion and is expected to reach $1.4 trillion by 2030. Finally, Tom Markusic, in a black t-shirt, stands in a workshop with a large rocket component, stating that 'Space is the next frontier for the information revolution' following the growth of the internet, as an animated visualization of Earth surrounded by a dense network of orbiting satellites, illustrating global connectivity, zooms out.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a dynamic animation of Earth surrounded by a dense network of orbiting satellites and space debris, emphasizing the crowded low-Earth orbit. A male voice, identified as Michael Sheetz, explains how services will be served from space, enabling more efficient information distribution and providing 'internet from the sky, internet everywhere.' The scene transitions to a SpaceX Falcon 9 rocket on a launch pad, followed by a satellite deploying its solar panels in orbit above a swirling storm system. Chad Anderson, speaking from a modern office with a space helmet behind him, elaborates that the billions spent in the past decade have primarily centered on building rockets or satellites, with steady growth in subsectors like suborbital space tourism, in-space manufacturing, and other data services. Michael Sheetz reappears, noting that companies are starting to go public or get acquired. A female voice then highlights high investor interest in SPACs, mentioning that both Virgin Orbit, shown as a white spaceplane carried under a carrier aircraft, and Rocket Lab, with its Electron rocket being assembled and then on a launch pad, have leveraged this to raise additional capital. Tom Markusic, Founder and CEO of Firefly Aerospace, seen in a workshop with large rocket sections, discusses going public as a way to fund development, suggesting a public offering for Firefly might be possible next year. The female voice concludes, stating that the company faces significant competition, with several startups aggressively fundraising and working to get rockets off the ground, as an aerial view of a launch site and a close-up of Astra’s small, silver rocket being assembled are shown.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment begins with a time-lapse exterior shot of a SpaceX facility at dusk, showing a tall white rocket illuminated by streetlights as cars create light trails. This transitions to an indoor interview where Chad Anderson, in a white shirt, speaks about tracking 'a little less than 100 companies that have been started by SpaceX alumni.' Next, Tom Markusic, Founder and CEO of Firefly Aerospace, appears in a workshop, stating in his voice that 'there are very few elite crowd that can actually do it' and that they will 'enter that elite stratosphere' with their second rocket. A dramatic rocket launch of a black and white Firefly rocket ignites and ascends, followed by control room engineers monitoring screens, while a female narrator's voice explains that 'once Firefly and others succeed in reaching space, they will be confronted with another challenge: building and refining operations for regular spaceflight.' The scene shifts to a workshop with large black rocket sections, where Lauren Lyons, a woman with curly hair, declares in her voice, 'We want to launch 24 rockets in 2024,' emphasizing the need to 'fly our next flight as soon as possible' to 'conquer that learning curve' and 'step into repeatable builds.' A 'The Verge' title card then announces a secret rocket engine IP deal between Astra and Firefly, as Chad Anderson's voice returns, stating that 'for them to be a viable business, they're going to need to get into other things.' Finally, Michael Sheetz, in a dark suit, speaks directly to the camera, with the female narrator's voice mentioning 'the company is reportedly in talks to provide engine technology to competing launch company Astra,' before Sheetz's voice concludes, 'The contract specified as many as 50 of the Reaver engines for Astra to use on their own.' The CNBC logo remains visible throughout.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with Tom Markusic, Founder and CEO of Firefly Aerospace, seated on a stool in a workshop, wearing a black 'FIREFLY' polo shirt, speaking directly to the camera about helping other companies and making money by sharing their rocket technology and future developments. A female narrator then explains that Firefly sees selling components as a big business, as visuals show a 3D model of a rocket engine and engineers working on a rocket component. As a large rocket is lifted by a yellow crane at a launch site, Tom Markusic elaborates on their vision for an e-commerce marketplace where customers can anonymously buy rocket components like engines and valves, similar to Amazon. He then appears in a different workshop, now in a black 'Blue Ghost' t-shirt with a lunar lander prototype behind him, using animated gestures to describe a future where Firefly components will be on most missions. The female narrator acknowledges the industry's inherent risks and tough road ahead, as the scene shifts to a control room where engineers, wearing headsets and masks, monitor screens. A male voice discusses the cyclical nature of rocket companies, experiencing both very low lows and very high highs.
